questions.text: In an INI inventory, how do you define a group of groups (nested groups)?
questions.explanation: In INI inventory, you create a group of groups using the ':children' suffix. For example, '[datacenter:children]' followed by group names allows you to target all hosts in both groups with a single group name.

questions.text: What inventory variable overrides the IP address/hostname used for the SSH connection?
questions.explanation: ansible_host overrides the address Ansible uses to connect to the host. This is useful when the inventory name is a label (e.g., 'db-primary') but the actual IP is different (e.g., 192.168.1.10).

citations.label: Connecting to hosts: behavioral inventory parameters

questions.text: Which special group contains ALL hosts in the inventory?
questions.explanation: The 'all' group is a built-in group that implicitly contains every host defined in the inventory. You can use it to apply variables to all hosts via group_vars/all.yml or target all hosts in a play with 'hosts: all'.

questions.text: Where should you place a file to set variables for the 'webservers' group?
questions.explanation: Ansible automatically loads variables from group_vars/<groupname>.yml (or group_vars/<groupname>/main.yml). Similarly, host_vars/<hostname>.yml is used for host-specific variables. These directories should be adjacent to your inventory file or playbook.

questions.text: Which ansible_connection value would you use for Windows managed nodes?
questions.explanation: For Windows nodes, ansible_connection should be set to 'winrm' (Windows Remote Management). This requires the pywinrm Python library. Alternatively, 'psrp' (PowerShell Remoting Protocol) can be used for better performance on Windows.

questions.text: In a YAML inventory, where do you define group-level variables?
questions.explanation: In YAML inventory format, group variables are defined under 'groupname.vars'. For example: webservers: vars: ansible_user: deploy. This is equivalent to creating a group_vars/webservers.yml file.

questions.text: Which special group contains hosts not assigned to any other group?
questions.explanation: The 'ungrouped' group automatically contains all hosts that have not been assigned to any custom group. Like 'all', it is a built-in group. Hosts in 'ungrouped' are still members of 'all'.

questions.text: How would you specify hosts web1 through web5 using Ansible's range pattern?
questions.explanation: Ansible supports numeric and alphabetic ranges in inventory using brackets: web[1:5].example.com or web[1-5].example.com (both work). You can also use alpha ranges: db[a:f].example.com. This expands to individual hostnames without listing each one.

questions.text: Variable precedence in Ansible: which takes higher priority?

options.text: They have equal priority, last-write wins

questions.explanation: Host variables (host_vars) take higher precedence than group variables (group_vars). Among group variables, child group vars take priority over parent group vars. This allows you to set defaults at the group level and override them per host.

questions.text: Which command lists all hosts in your inventory?
questions.explanation: ansible-inventory --list outputs your entire inventory as JSON, showing all hosts and groups. Use --graph for a more readable tree view. This is especially useful for debugging dynamic inventories and verifying that host/group variables are set correctly.
